blurb: The most refined native bunchgrass: an emerald fountain of hair-fine leaves, airy seedheads that famously smell of coriander and popcorn, and glowing apricot-gold fall color.
size: 2–3 ft tall in seed × 2 ft wide (foliage mound ~18 in)
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Emerald fine fountain in summer; airy fragrant seedheads late summer; apricot-gold fall fading to light bronze in winter
wildlife: Seeds feed birds; larval host; warm-season prairie grass

care.hardiness: Extremely cold-hardy (USDA zones 3–8), a native warm-season prairie bunchgrass whose range runs from the cold northern plains south into Colorado, so it sails through Boulder winters (zone 5b–6a) with no protection.[2][3] It is the most refined of the native bunchgrasses and very long-lived once settled in.[1]
care.planting: Set out container plants or plugs in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, while soil is workable; because it is a warm-season grass it puts on its main growth in the heat of summer, so don't expect much top growth until soil warms. In pots it wants a gritty, free-draining mix and protection from soggy winter wet, which rots the crown.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun for the best, densest form and fall color; it tolerates light shade but grows looser there.[1]
care.soil: Adaptable and unfussy — it grows in clay, loam, sand, and shallow rocky soils, including Boulder's alkaline clay, provided drainage is good. It is at home on dry prairies and well-drained sites and dislikes wet feet.[3][1]
care.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established thanks to a deep, fibrous root system. Water occasionally to establish, then little to no supplemental water is needed.[1][3]
care.spacing: Allow about 2–3 ft between plants, matching its mature 2–3 ft width, so each clump can form its full fountain.[3]
care.propagation: Best bought as plugs or container plants. It is slow and difficult from seed (germination is erratic, and a transplant takes about three seasons to reach specimen size), and the tight crown is tough and slow to divide — division is the recommended vegetative route but is hard work and recovers slowly.[1][3]
care.sow: If growing from seed, sow unstratified seed outdoors in fall, or sow dry-stratified seed in spring, at about 1/4 in deep; collect ripe seed in October.[1]
care.stratify: Seed benefits from a dry-stratification (dry chilling) treatment before a spring sowing; a fall sowing lets winter provide the chill naturally.[1]
care.bloom: Airy, fragrant flower/seedheads — famously scented of coriander and buttered popcorn — open in summer (roughly June–August) and persist into fall.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low maintenance: cut the clump back to a few inches in late winter before new spring growth, and otherwise leave it be. The foliage turns apricot-gold in fall, fading to light bronze, and the clump holds its form through winter for structure.[1][3]
care.selfsow: A tight, well-behaved, non-running clumper that does not spread aggressively; it may self-sow modestly but is easily kept in bounds.[3]
care.troubles: No serious pests or diseases; the main risk is crown rot in wet, poorly drained, or over-irrigated soil.[3]

edible.summary: A minor, historic grain: the seeds were used by some Native peoples to make flour, though it was never a major staple.[3]
edible.parts: The small seeds, borne on the fragrant airy seedheads in late summer. Documented food use is limited — primarily noted as a flour source — so treat it as a minor or emergency grain, not a productive food crop.[3]
edible.preparation: Where used, the ripe seeds were gathered and ground into flour/meal; the seeds are very small and the per-plant yield is low, so it is labor-intensive for little return.[3]
edible.caution: Documented food use for this species is thin (its better-documented Native uses are medicinal), so be conservative — be certain of your identification, harvest only clean ripe seed, and discard any seedheads showing dark, hardened ergot-like fungal bodies that can infect grass florets.[3][2]
